coredata.dc:title: Electron-rich silicon quantum dots-based charge transfer probe for highly selective chemiluminescence detection of Fe2+ in PM2.5
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Houjing
Mo, Tingting
Zhou, Yu
Gong, Hui
Zhao, Dayang
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) assist chemiluminescence (CL) sensor, especially metal-free QDs, are currently attracting considerable attention in the establishment of new CL systems to expand their analytical applications with good biocompatibility, controllable properties, and easy functionalization, while the fundamental CL process from QDs are still far from clear by now. Herein, A strategy of constructing CL probe is developed by carefully studying the charge transfer process in the electron-rich QDs. We purposefully synthesized silicon QDs (SiQDs) with electron-rich property, which can react with hole injector (oxidants) forming extraordinary CL emission. According to the characterization of reactive intermediates and emitting species, a possible CL mechanism was proposed, where radiative recombination of the exogenous holes-injected and existing electrons SiQDs resulted in the surprising SiQDs CL behavior. Interestingly, when Fe2+ were added, more exogenous holes and active sites were formed on the surface of SiQDs during Fe2+/Fe3+ conversion, which can significantly amplify CL signal. On this basis, a new CL system for the detection of ferrous in airborne fine particulate matter (PM2.5) was constructed. This work provides a charge transfer-based path for designing a simple and highly sensitive CL probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Electron-rich
Silicon quantum dots
Ferrous
PM2.5

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China is appreciatively acknowledged (Nos. 22104023). This work was also supported by Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology projects (Nos. ZK [2022] General 078).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) the size distribution histogram, (C) survey XPS spectrum and (D) FT-IR spectrum of SiQDs.

(A) CL intensity − time profiles of SiQDs and oxidant, containing KMnO4 (green curve), H2O2 (burgundy curve), NaClO (black curve) and O2 (red curve). (B) CL emission of SiQDs in the presence of Fe2+, Fe3+ and common mental ions (the inset of B). Conditions: the concentration of SiQDs and mental ions were 100 mg/mL and 1 mM, respectively.

(A) The inhibition percentage of different scavengers on the CL of SiQDs/Fe2+system. (B) Time-dependent absorption spectra of the NBT in different concentration of SiQDs and Fe2+ system. (C-D) EPR spectra of ·OH and 1O2 generating in the SiQDs/Fe2+ system.

(A) the linear curve between CL intensity and the concentration of Fe2+ (0.5–500 μ M). (B) The effect of possible coexisting compounds in PM2.5 on the CL intensity of SiQDs/Fe2+ system. (C) The CL response of SiQDs toward different atmospheric particulates suspension. The concentration of SiQDs is 200 mg/mL.
Schematic illustration of the CL mechanism in the SiQDs/Fe2+ system.

head.ce:title: Electron-rich silicon quantum dots-based charge transfer probe for highly selective chemiluminescence detection of Fe2+ in PM2.5

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) assist chemiluminescence (CL) sensor, especially metal-free QDs, are currently attracting considerable attention in the establishment of new CL systems to expand their analytical applications with good biocompatibility, controllable properties, and easy functionalization, while the fundamental CL process from QDs are still far from clear by now. Herein, A strategy of constructing CL probe is developed by carefully studying the charge transfer process in the electron-rich QDs. We purposefully synthesized silicon QDs (SiQDs) with electron-rich property, which can react with hole injector (oxidants) forming extraordinary CL emission. According to the characterization of reactive intermediates and emitting species, a possible CL mechanism was proposed, where radiative recombination of the exogenous holes-injected and existing electrons SiQDs resulted in the surprising SiQDs CL behavior. Interestingly, when Fe2+ were added, more exogenous holes and active sites were formed on the surface of SiQDs during Fe2+/Fe3+ conversion, which can significantly amplify CL signal. On this basis, a new CL system for the detection of ferrous in airborne fine particulate matter (PM2.5) was constructed. This work provides a charge transfer-based path for designing a simple and highly sensitive CL probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is a molecular luminescence phenomenon that is produced by radiative transition in the process of oxidation–reduction or electron transfer.[1,2] As a very attractive and effective analytical method, it has been widely used in various fields due to its advantages of low background interference, wide detection range, easy miniaturization of equipment, fast detection speed, and simple setup.[3,4] In particular, with the progress of nanotechnology, quantum dots (QDs) have brought new development opportunities to build new CL systems and expand the application of CL analysis through surface state or charge radiative recombination.[5–7] In the annihilation mechanism, electron donors inject electron into the conduction band (CB) of QD, producing QD with a negative charge (QD·-), and oxidants excite the generated holes of QDs valence band (VB), generating QD with a negative charge (QD·+). Then, the recombination of QD·- and QD·+ results in the formation of excited state QDs, which can generate strong luminescence by electron-transfer recombination[8,9]. Limited by the charge transfer efficiency, more and more efforts have been devoted to develop and construct new strategies including electrode[10], co-reactant[11] and regulation of band gap[12] to enhance the CL signal. Thus, developing a general route to trigger QDs-based CL probe with high charge transfer efficiency to broaden its analytical applications and enhance its sensitivity is of great significance and full of challenges.

Recently, metal-free QDs, especially pure elemental QDs, have attracted great attention in the analytical field because of their good biocompatibility, controllable properties, and easy functionalization[13–15]. Silicon QDs (SiQDs), as an emerging metal-free nanomaterial, have attracted considerable attention among analysts due to their lower toxicity and excellent optical and electronic properties [16,17]. In recent years, SiQDs were used as luminaries or carriers to participate in electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and fluorescence (FL) reactions [18,19]. ECL and FL investigations demonstrated that SiQDs can be injected with charges through light, electricity, and chemical injection by strong reductants and/or oxidants [20–22]. Recently, we found that the electron transfer efficiency can be tuned by engineering the energy band gap of SiQDs and successfully established bisulfite sensing in atmospheric particles. Additionally, Lv’s group conducted some pioneering works on this area. They successfully fabricated silicon carbon nanoparticles (SiC NPs)-based CL systems and applied them to selectively detect reactive oxygen species (ROS) in PM2.5.[23] The research of CL mechanism of QDs proved that the radiative annihilation between the electrons in the CB of n-type phosphors and the exogenous holes injection by hydroxyl radical (·OH), account for CL emission. However, the report focused on the intrinsic luminescence mechanism and general routes of SiQDs, and the correlation between SiQDs nanostructures and CL performance remain unknown.

In environmental, Fe2+ are trace element, as one of the most important components in PM2.5, which play an important role in environment and biological process. Toxicologicalresearch showed that abnormal levels of Fe2+ disturbed the ecological balance leading to a series of chronic diseases. [24–26] Hence, finding a direct and accurate method for Fe2+ detection in PM2.5 is still ongoing. To date, several techniques have been developed to determination Fe2+ in various matrices, including chromatographic,[27] electrochemical,[28] spectroscopic,[29–31] colorimetric[32] and fluorescent[33–36]. However, those methods are severely limitedbecause oflengthy pretreatment sample procedures, expensive instrumentation, or toxic reagents. Several CL assays based on organic molecules have been devised for detecting Fe2+, [37–39] which were greatly limited for practical applications due to the interference from other divalent cations and poisonous reagents. To overcome the limitation, Lin’s group developed nanoparticles-based CL probe utilizing nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) toward Fe2+ with outstanding practicability [40]. Inspired by the limited number of pioneering works, we aim to explore new nanomaterial-based CL assay for Fe2+ and the intrinsic mechanism between the nanomaterial’s structure and CL performance.

In present work, we purposefully fabricated an electron-rich SiQDs based CL sensing platform for selective andsensitive detection of Fe2+ in PM2.5, which has satisfactory sensitivity and anti-interference capability from Fe3+ and other cations. Further research was performed to propose the CL mechanism that the chemically exogenous holes (oxidizing agents) recombined with the existing electrons of SiQDs producing CL emission. Additionally, an enhanced CL signal of SiQDs was observed after the mixing of ferrous ions due to the generation of more highly catalytic active sites and hole injector. This work has provided a new strategy to construct highly sensitive CL probes, and further expanded the practical application of SiQDs in the field of analysis.

All reagents were at least of analytical grade, unless otherwise specified. The solution was prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ/cm), which was obtained by a specialized instrument (a Milli-QULUPURE, Chengdu, China). N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO) and M-phenylenediamine (m-PD) were obtained from Macklin company (Shanghai, China). 2,2,6,6tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride (NBT), thiourea, ascorbic acid, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), FeCl2 and FeCl3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent company. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were provided by the Keyi Chemical Reagent company (Guiyang China).

All CL signals were recorded using the ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (BPCL-2-TGC, Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, PRC). The FL spectra measurement was completed on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Inc., Palo Alto, USA) equipped with a 650 V photomultiplier voltage using the following parameters: scan speed 600 nm min−1, 5 nm excitation and emission slit widths. The Shimadzu UV-2700 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, China) was used to measure the UV–vis absorption of materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha with copper target Kα rays (λ = 0.15406 nm). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (200KV)) imagines were used to analyze the morphology of materials. IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR, Thermo, USA) was used to analyze functional groups. The FL lifetime decay measurements were carried out on using the Edinburgh FLS1000 to analyze the change of materials. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were used to studied reactive species using a Bruker E-500 instrument. Conditions: microwave power 12.72 mW, microwave frequency 9.77 GHz, modulation frequency 100 kHz, and sweep width 3480 G).

To demonstrate the formation of SiQDs (the synthetic process of SiQDs is shown in the ESI), we performed the TEM and XPS measurement. The TEM imagines indicated that SiQDs were well dispersed with spherical shape, and distributed in the range of 4–6 nm with an average dimension of 2.3 nm (Fig. 1 A-B). The XPS survey scan spectrum of SiQDs (Fig. 1C) showed the chemical composition of materials with five characteristic peaks O1s, N1s, C1s, Si2s, and Si2p, which were apparent at 532.08 eV, 399.08 eV, 285.08 eV, 153.08 eV, and 102.08 eV respectively. To investigate the surface elemental composition and valence states of obtained samples, we analyzed FT-IR spectra and the high-resolution XPS spectra in specifically the Si, C, N and O regions (Fig. 1D and Fig. S1). In the high-resolution Si 2p spectrum, two characteristic peaks (Fig. S1A) at around 101.5 eV and 102.1 eV were ascribed to the Si-C and Si-N, which lines of Si-based QDs binding energy [41]. The high-resolution N 1s, O 1s and C 1s spectra were further to identify the surface functional groups [42]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. S1B) centering at 398.4 eV, 399 eV and 400.4 eV are assigning to N-Si, N-C and N-H, respectively. There binding energy peaks at 284.6 eV, 284 eV and 285.2 eV revealed C-C, C-Si and C-N binding energy, respectively (Fig. S1C). In addition, the two peaks at 532.2 eV and 531.4 eV from the O 1 s spectrum could be attributed to C-O and C-OH, respectively [41] (Fig. S1D). These finding confirmed the functional groups modified on the surface of SiQDs with –NH2, –OH, etc. Subsequent FTIR analysis further demonstrated the above result. The vibrational peaks at 1593 cm−1, 3274 cm−1, 785 cm−1 and 695 cm−1 are from N-H bond, and the signals centering at 1128 cm−1 and 1034 cm−1 are originated from the stretching vibration of C-O bonding. The peaks at 920 cm−1 and 1211 cm−1 are assigned to Si–H and C–Si bonds. All of the result confirmed that some functional groups were introduced into QDs structure, which probably modulated the energy band gap and accelerate electron transfer on the surface of QDs, resulting in the improvement of luminescence performances of SiQDs and show great potential to be a promising candidate for an ideal optical probe.

The optical properties of materials also reflect their subtle structural characteristics and is important to explain the chemical reaction mechanism. We conducted UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra of SiQDs. The UV–vis spectrum of SiQDs shows (Fig. S2A) an obviously absorption band centered at 380 nm, which indicated that the existence of an unsaturated bond originated from the π-π* transition of Si = C bond [43]. The FL emission of SiQDs change significantly under illumination of Xe lamp with different wavelengths, and the FL intensity obtained a strongest peak at 480 nm under the 370 nm excitation (Fig. S2B), the above phenomenon is similar to the published reports.[44] Inspired by the excellent luminescence properties, we aim to explore its promising application in CL sensing.

To explore the CL mechanism of the SiQDs-Fe2+ system, we initially investigated the emitting species during the SiQDs/Fe2+ reaction. The CL spectrum showed a broad band peaked at 505 nm (Fig. S4D), which was very close to that of FL emission. This finding implied that SiQDs show great potential as emitter in the SiQDs /Fe2+ CL system. Of note, compared with the maximum PL of SiQDs, the slight red-shift was probably due to the smaller energy band gap from the SiQDs surface states [45]. Thus, we inferred that the CL emission probably account to the various surface energy traps from SiQDs. Subsequently, we analyzed the possible intermediates during the CL reaction to understand the free radical transfer in the SiQDs/Fe2+ CL system. Here, six kinds of free radical quenchers were selected to determinate the possible reactive intermediates, namely, AA, DMSO, NBT, thiourea, l-hisditine and ethanol. Among them, AA can quench most free radicals [46]. l-hisditine has the most obvious quenching effect on 1O2.[47] NBT has a significant quenching effect on O2 ∙-.[48] Thiourea, ethanol, and DMSO are usually used as inhibitors of ∙OH.[49] As shown in Fig. 3 A. AA can completely quench the CL signals of the SiQDs /Fe2+ system at low concentration (1 mM), which indicates that free radicals in this system mainly participate in the CL reaction. Then, to verify the effect of ∙OH on the CL system, thiourea, DMSO and ethanol were selected for the experiment, The CL intensity does not change with the change of thiourea, DMSO, and ethanol concentration, which shows that ∙OH was not important for CL emission. l-histidine (1 mM) has a certain inhibition on the CL intensity. Therefore, it can be inferred that 1O2 has limited contribution to CL emission. O2 ∙- is often the precursor of other free radicals and occupies an important position in the process of free radical transformation. As a specific O2 ∙- capture agent, NBT is widely used in the field of analysis. The CL intensity of the system was significantly inhibited by 10 mM NBT, indicating that O2 ∙- plays an important role in CL reaction. Moreover, the absorption signal for SiQDs/Fe2+/NBT significantly improved when the concentration of SiQDs was increased (Fig. 3B). The result proved that SiQDs accelerate the production of O2 ∙-. Therefore, through the CL quenching experiments of the system, it is found that O2 ∙- has a great influence on the CL signals, whereas 1O2 has a certain influence on it.

Although the CL quenching experiment can infer the effect of major free radical species during CL reaction, it also has some limitations in selectivity and sensitivity. Whether there are other free radicals or active species affecting CL signals remains unclear. In addition, these free radical quenchers are reducing substances, which can identify free radical species through the different reaction rates between quenchers and free radicals. CL reaction is usually the result of the interaction of multiple free radicals, and there is mutual transformation between free radicals. Therefore, other method was needed to be used to prove these finding. EPR is a direct and effective method to study the mechanism of free radicals in chemical reactions. Free radical with paramagnetic properties are formed with trapping agents, which have obvious characteristic peaks under the action of magnetic field. DMPO is used as the capture agent of · OH. The DMPO-OH adduct presents a characteristic peak of 1:2:2:1.[50] The information of OH and relative content can be identified according to its peak type, peak position, peak intensity. On this basis, DMPO was selected as a free radical capture agent for EPR characterization, so as to identify the species and content of free radicals in the SiQDs-Fe2+ system. The typically ESR signals of DMPO-OH adduct were not observed in SiQDs-Fe2+ mixture (Fig. 3C), indicating that ·OH was not generated. Moreover, the production of 1O2 during CL emission was confirmed by TEMP.[51] A characteristic spectra of TEMPO is clearly observed (Fig. 3D), which demonstrated the presence of 1O2 in the SiQDs solution. SiQDs exhibit abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and high electron transfer efficiency, suggesting that SiQDs be used as a catalyst to accelerate the oxidization of dissolved oxygen to form O2 ∙-, resulting a large amount of 1O2. The speculation that a weak CL signal was observed when oxygen was introduced into the solution was proved (Fig. 2B). The effect of dissolved oxygen also demonstrated above speculation (Table 1S). Moreover, the addition of Fe2+ decreased the ESR intensity, proving the reduction of 1O2. To further verify the interaction between SiQDs and Fe2+, we also monitored the Fe2+ in the reaction process in real time. 1,10-Phenanthroline can combine with Fe2+ to form an orange complex with a characteristic UV–vis absorption peak at 510 nm, which was introduced into the system to explore the change of Fe2+ content before and after the addition of SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S5A, in the presence of SiQDs, there is no obvious absorption peak at 510 nm in Fe2+ solution after adding 1,10-phenanthroline, indicating the consumption of Fe2+. Interestingly, an obvious absorption peak was observed in the SiQDs-Fe2+ solution after 10 min, which shows that Fe2+ was regenerate in the system with the addition of SiQDs. Then, the absorbance of the mixture of 1,10-phenanthroline, Fe3+ and different concentrations of SiQDs was studied. With the enhancement of SiQDs concentration (Fig. S5B), the absorption intensity gradually increases, and the color changes from colorless to orange, which confirmed that SiQDs reacted with Fe2+ to form Fe3+ and then effectively converts Fe3+ into Fe2+. A weak signal from the SiQDs-Fe3+ system also demonstrated the above speculation (Fig. 2C). SiQDs accelerate the conversion of Fe3+ into Fe2+ in the system and increase the utilization efficiency of Fe2+, which make it have great potential in the field of dye degradation. We found that the SiQDs-Fe2+ CL system actually has great application potential in the degradation of BRB4 and amaranth dyes (Fig. S4C-D).

XPS characterization and contrasted FT-IR measurement of SiQDs were also carried out using Fe2+ to investigate the SiQDs-Fe2+ CL mechanism. Based on the XPS survey scan spectrum showing in (Fig. S6A), a new characteristic peak corresponding to the Fe element was observed, indicating that Fe-containing chemical bonds were formed during the reaction process of Fe2+ and SiQDs. Subsequently, the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectra of the SiQDs/ Fe2+ complex (Fig. S6B) revealed four peaks at 708.3 eV (Fe 2p3/2), 722.0 eV (Fe 2p1/2), 710.6 eV (N-coordinated Fe3+/Fe2+) and 714.0 eV (statellite of Fe3+/Fe2+), thus indicating the presence of Fe3+/Fe2+ and the formation of catalytic active Fe-Nx sites. For the high-resolution O 2p and Si 2p XPS spectra (Fig. S6C-D), a new Si-O chemical bond was formed in contrast to SiQDs, indicating that the Si-O groups were involved in the circulation of Fe ions. The FTIR spectra of SiQDs/Fe2+ and SiQDs also confirmed the above speculation. As shown in Fig. S7, a new absorption peak at ∼900 cm−1 was observed, which corresponds to the Si-OH bond in SiQDs/Fe2+.

According to the above finding, the possible CL mechanism of the SiQDs/Fe2+ system is illustrated as follows (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, the surface functional groups of SiQDs react with dissolved oxygen to formed O2 ·- (reaction (1)).[52] O2 ·- easily converted into 1O2 and –OH (reaction (2)).[53] When Fe2+ was added, the formation of catalytic active Fe-Nx sites and Fe3+/Fe2+ conversion further promoted the generation of O2 ·-, 1O2 and SiQDs (h+ injection)+ (reaction 3–4). O2 ·- also injected electrons into the CB of SiQDs (reaction (5)), and the chemically injected exogenous holes recombined with pre-existing endogenous conduction band (CB) electrons generated excited-state SiQDs (SiQDs*), which converted into ground state and generated light at 505 nm (reaction 6–7). 1O2 is also unneglectable during SiQDs/Fe2+ CL process, which probably originated from transfer energy between excited dimer (1O2)2* (emission 381, 477, 578 nm) [53,54] and SiQDs forming 505 nm light emission (reaction (8)).

We further explore its potential as a CL reagent for Fe2+ discrimination. To pursue lower detection limit and wider calibration range, the effects of SiQDs concentration, pH value, and PBS concentration were investigated. Fig. S8A-B shows that the best CL response was obtained in the condition of pH = 2, PBS concentration of 0.02 mol/L and SiQDs concentration of 200 mg/mL. Under the optimal conditions, CL signals exhibits wide linear relationship with Fe2+ concentrations (0.5 μM to 500 μM) with the detection limit (LOD) (S/N = 3) down to 0.217 μM, which is comparable to those in previous reports (Table 2S). The regression equation of the plotted calibration graph is I = 117.93 [Fe2+] + 704.54 (R = 0.9936) (Fig. 4 A), Furthermore, we applied this CL assay into complex samples detection. We studied the anti-interference capability of proposed CL assay. The possible coexisting substances in PM2.5, [55] including Cu2+, Mg2+, Li+, Mn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ti4+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu+, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, F-, S2O3 2-, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, MoO4 2-, and C2O4 2-. Fig. 4B shows that the CL response of SiQDs to Fe2+ was impervious to these coexisting ions, demonstrating that the proposed sensing platform have advantage of high selectivity toward Fe2+ and is great potential for the detection of Fe2+ in atmospheric particulates. On the basis of the superior characteristics of this CL assay, a spike recovery test was used to determinethe response for matrix in PM2.5 samples of SiQDs as a CL probe. The appearance of a little large standard error in actual samples possibly originated from existence of oxidizing species, which oxidize Fe2 + ions. And the actual sample dissolution needs to be treated with nitrogen and reducing agent to remove dissolved oxygen or other oxidizing substances. Finally, we can observe a representative PM2.5 sample (0.52 mg/mL) with obvious CL responds Fig. 4C, and good recoveries (91–105 %) of spiked Fe2+ species were obtained (Table 3S), suggesting a great potential for the detection of Fe2+ in PM2.5.

We purposefully synthesized SiQDs with electron-rich property, which can react with oxidants resulting in extraordinary CL emission. Based on our experiments, the CL mechanism demonstrated that oxidants injected holes into the VB of SiQDs, and the chemically injected exogenous holes recombined with pre-existing endogenous CB electrons, producing high CL emission. Additionally, when ferrous was added, more highly catalytic active sites were formed on the surface of SiQDs, resulting in the generation of a large amount of ROS and amplification of CL signal. On this basis, a sensitive and selective CL assay for Fe2+ detection in PM2.5 was developed. This work provides a new route to design CL nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity, which shows great application value in the detection of practical complex samples.

Houjing Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration. Tingting Mo: Data curation, Investigation. Yu Zhou: Formal analysis. Hui Gong: Writing – review & editing. Dayang Zhao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China is appreciatively acknowledged (Nos. 22104023). This work was also supported by Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology projects (Nos. ZK [2022] General 078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, X. R.; Garcia‐Campana, A. M.; Ouyang. J., Recent developments in chemiluminescence sensors. Trends Anal. Chem. 1999, 18, 384-391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies in liquid-phase chemiluminescence and their applications in bioassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Su, Y. Y.; Deng, D. Y.; Zhang, L. C.; Song, H. J.; Lv, Y., Strategies in liquid-phase chemiluminescence and their applications in bioassay. Trends Anal. Chem. 2016, 82, 394-411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-(Aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol) and hemin dual-functionalized graphene hybrids with high chemiluminescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-involved chemiluminescence: Recent advances and developments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, D. M.; Lin, K. L.; Huang, C. Z., Carbon dots-involved chemiluminescence: Recent advances and developments. Luminescence 2019, 34, 4-22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nitride quantum dots: a novel chemiluminescence system for selective detection of free chlorine in water

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MoS2-quantum dot triggered reactive oxygen species generation and depletion: responsible for enhanced chemiluminescence
